shortDescription: Surgical resurfacing of a worn knee joint to restore pain-free movement and stable walking.

sections.overview:
Knee replacement removes the damaged cartilage and bone surfaces of an arthritic or injured knee and replaces them with metal and high-grade plastic implants, restoring smoother movement and reducing pain. Patients from Africa, the Middle East, and Asia often choose Korea because accredited Korean hospitals combine modern implant systems, robotic-assisted alignment tools, and structured early physiotherapy under one coordinated pathway.

The operation is usually recommended when osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, or post-injury joint damage causes persistent pain that limits walking, climbing stairs, sleeping, or daily independence, and when non-surgical measures such as medication, weight management, and physiotherapy no longer give adequate relief. Surgeons assess whether the whole joint is affected or only one compartment, which guides the choice between a total or partial replacement.

A total knee replacement resurfaces all three compartments of the joint, while a partial (unicompartmental) replacement treats only the worn section and preserves healthy ligaments and bone. Modern Korean centres frequently use robotic-assisted planning to position implants according to each patient's anatomy, which supports balanced ligaments and even load through the new joint. Most implants are fixed with bone cement, and rehabilitation begins within a day of surgery to protect range of motion and muscle strength.

KmedTour coordinates the full journey, including pre-arrival imaging review, interpreter support, hospital admission, and a written rehabilitation plan you can hand to a physiotherapist at home. The goal is a comfortable, well-aligned joint and a clear, realistic recovery path, planned around your travel dates and your home-country follow-up so that progress continues safely after you return.
sections.candidacy:
You may be a candidate for knee replacement if you have moderate to severe knee arthritis or joint damage with daily pain, stiffness, or instability that has not responded to medication, injections, weight management, or physiotherapy. Imaging that shows significant cartilage loss, bone-on-bone changes, or deformity helps confirm the need for surgery. Candidates are usually adults whose pain limits walking, stairs, work, or sleep.

A partial replacement may suit patients whose damage is confined to one compartment with intact ligaments, while widespread joint disease usually points toward a total replacement. Good candidates are well enough for anaesthesia and committed to the rehabilitation that follows.

Before approving surgery, the team reviews your general health, including heart and lung fitness, diabetes control, and any blood-clotting or bleeding history. Active infection, very poor bone quality, or unstable medical conditions may delay surgery until they are managed. Smoking and uncontrolled blood sugar can slow healing, so the team may ask you to address these first. A pre-travel video consultation and your recent scans allow the surgeon to confirm suitability and plan implant choice before you fly.
sections.procedure_steps:
On the day of surgery you receive either a general anaesthetic or a spinal anaesthetic, often with a nerve block to ease pain afterward. The surgeon opens the front of the knee and exposes the joint.

In a total knee replacement, the worn ends of the thighbone and shinbone are reshaped, and the surfaces are replaced with a metal femoral component and a metal tibial tray separated by a smooth plastic spacer; the kneecap may also be resurfaced. In a partial (unicompartmental) replacement, only the single damaged compartment is resurfaced, sparing the healthy bone and ligaments, which can mean a smaller incision and quicker early recovery.

Many Korean hospitals use robotic-assisted or computer-navigated alignment. The surgeon plans implant position from a digital model of your knee, and the system guides bone preparation so the components sit in balanced alignment with even ligament tension. Most implants are fixed with bone cement, giving immediate stability that allows early weight-bearing.

Throughout, the team balances the soft tissues so the knee bends and straightens smoothly and tracks correctly. Once the implant is tested through a full range of motion, the wound is closed in layers and dressed, and measures to prevent blood clots begin straight away. The operation typically takes one to two hours, and you are moved to recovery before returning to the ward, where physiotherapy is planned to start within the first day.
sections.recovery_timeline:
Recovery is structured around your fourteen-day stay. You will usually stand and take guided steps with a walking frame within the first day, and physiotherapy focuses on bending and straightening the knee, building quadriceps strength, and safe walking. Pain control, wound checks, and clot-prevention measures continue throughout admission.

During the first week, the surgical team monitors the wound, manages swelling, and progresses your walking distance and stair practice. Stitches or clips are checked or removed around the end of the second week, and the surgeon confirms that the wound is healing and the joint is moving well before clearing you to travel.

Most patients are advised not to fly until the surgeon confirms it is safe, generally toward the end of the two-week stay, because longer flights carry a clot risk after major joint surgery. You will receive guidance on compression, hydration, moving your ankles during the flight, and any prescribed blood-thinning medication to continue.

Before departure, KmedTour provides a written rehabilitation plan, your operative summary, and implant details so a physiotherapist at home can continue your program. Recovery continues for several weeks to a few months as strength, range of motion, and walking endurance improve. Your home physiotherapist supervises ongoing exercises, and you should arrange a local follow-up review. Report increasing pain, redness, fever, or calf swelling promptly, as these need prompt assessment.
sections.cost_breakdown:
The estimated cost for knee replacement through KmedTour ranges from $12,000 to $25,000. Where you fall in this range depends on whether you need a total or partial replacement, the implant system selected, whether robotic-assisted alignment is used, and the length of your hospital stay.

The quoted price generally covers the surgeon and anaesthesia fees, the implant, operating-room use, your inpatient stay, standard medications, routine post-operative imaging, and inpatient physiotherapy during admission. Pre-operative tests and assessments are usually included once you arrive.

Items normally not included are international flights, accommodation before or after your hospital stay, meals outside the hospital, and any extended rehabilitation or follow-up care once you return home. If both knees are treated or additional procedures are required, the cost will be higher.

KmedTour provides a written, itemised quotation after the surgeon reviews your scans, so you can see what is and is not covered before you commit. We also help arrange interpreter support and local accommodation so you can budget for the full trip rather than the surgery alone.
sections.why_korea:
Korea is a well-established destination for orthopaedic surgery, with major hospitals accredited under national and international standards and overseen within a healthcare system regulated by the Ministry of Health and Welfare. KOIHA accreditation and KHIDI's support for international patients reflect a country-level focus on quality and patient safety for visitors.

Many large Korean hospitals offer robotic-assisted and computer-navigated joint replacement, which supports precise implant alignment planned around each patient's anatomy. These centres typically run high-volume orthopaedic and rehabilitation services, with physiotherapy integrated into the surgical pathway from the first day after surgery.

For international patients, Korean hospitals provide coordinated international patient departments, interpreter services, and structured discharge planning. KmedTour adds end-to-end coordination, from pre-arrival imaging review to a written rehabilitation handover for your home physiotherapist, so that care planned in Korea continues smoothly once you return to your home country.

faqs.q: How will I continue physiotherapy after I return home?
faqs.a: Before you leave Korea, KmedTour gives you a written rehabilitation plan, your operative summary, and implant details to hand to a local physiotherapist. Your exercises continue at home under their supervision, gradually building strength, range of motion, and walking endurance. We recommend arranging a follow-up review with a local orthopaedic clinic so progress is monitored and any concerns are checked in person.

faqs.q: Is it safe to fly home with a new knee joint?
faqs.a: Your surgeon will confirm when flying is safe, usually toward the end of the fourteen-day stay, once the wound is healing and the joint is moving well. Long flights carry a higher clot risk after major joint surgery, so you will receive guidance on compression, staying hydrated, moving your ankles regularly, and continuing any prescribed blood-thinning medication during the journey.

faqs.q: What is done to prevent blood clots and DVT?
faqs.a: Clot prevention begins straight after surgery and continues through your stay and travel. Measures may include early walking, ankle movements, compression, staying hydrated, and prescribed blood-thinning medication. The team will tell you how long to continue any anticoagulant after you return home. Report calf pain, swelling, redness, or breathlessness promptly, as these can signal a clot that needs prompt assessment.

faqs.q: What is the difference between a total and a partial knee replacement?
faqs.a: A total knee replacement resurfaces all three compartments of the joint and suits widespread arthritis. A partial, or unicompartmental, replacement treats only one worn section while preserving healthy bone and ligaments, which can mean a smaller incision and faster early recovery. The surgeon decides based on your imaging and how much of the joint is affected, confirming the plan before surgery.

faqs.q: How long until I can walk normally after surgery?
faqs.a: Most patients stand and take guided steps with a frame within the first day, then progress to walking and stairs during the stay. Comfortable everyday walking usually returns over several weeks as swelling settles and strength builds, with full recovery taking a few months. Consistent physiotherapy at home is the main driver of steady progress, so following your plan closely matters.
